name: Alexa Always Listening
description: The 'Alexa Always Listening' theory posits that Amazon's Alexa, and similar smart home devices, are constantly recording and analyzing conversations even when not actively triggered by the wake word. Proponents believe this data is used for targeted advertising, data mining, government surveillance, or even manipulation. The theory gained traction with the rise of smart home technology and increasing concerns about data privacy.  Evidence cited includes anecdotal experiences of users seeing targeted ads related to conversations held near Alexa devices, as well as concerns about Amazon's privacy policies and data collection practices. Some point to documented instances of Alexa mistakenly recording conversations or sending recordings to unintended recipients as proof of potential vulnerabilities and malicious intent. Mainstream perspective is that while Alexa devices are technically always 'listening' for the wake word, they are not continuously recording and storing audio. Amazon states that audio is only recorded after the wake word is detected and is encrypted and stored securely.  Security experts acknowledge the potential for vulnerabilities and privacy breaches but argue that these are typically due to software bugs or user error rather than intentional surveillance. Regulatory bodies have investigated privacy concerns surrounding smart home devices and have issued guidelines for data protection.  The debate highlights the tension between technological convenience, data privacy, and public trust in large tech corporations.
subtitle: Smart Home Surveillance Conspiracy Theory

claims.claim: Alexa records conversations even without the wake word.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal experiences of targeted ads related to offline conversations near Alexa devices.
claims.criticism: Amazon denies this and claims recordings only occur after the wake word. Targeted ads can arise from other data sources and algorithms.

claims.claim: Amazon uses Alexa data for mass surveillance.
claims.evidence: Concerns about Amazon's partnership with law enforcement and government agencies.
claims.criticism: Amazon states that it only provides data in response to valid legal requests and follows strict privacy guidelines.

claims.claim: Alexa can be hacked to continuously record audio.
claims.evidence: Documented security vulnerabilities in smart home devices.
claims.criticism: While vulnerabilities exist, Amazon and other companies release security patches and updates to address them. Exploitation requires technical expertise.

keyFigures.name: Shoshana Zuboff
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Age of Surveillance Capitalism', critiquing data collection practices.

keyFigures.name: Amazon Security Team
keyFigures.role: Responsible for defending Alexa against security threats and maintaining user privacy.

timeline.event: Amazon releases the first Echo device with Alexa.
Reports of Alexa mistakenly recording and sending private conversations.
Growing public concern about data privacy and the potential for smart home surveillance.

sources.author: Zuboff, Shoshana
sources.work: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism: The Fight for a Human Future at the New Frontier of Power
sources.title: Zuboff, Shoshana

sources.author: Consumer Reports
sources.work: How to Protect Your Privacy With Smart Speakers
sources.title: Consumer Reports
